coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence probe for selectively monitoring biothiols within cells and mouse depression diagnosis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Junyan
Xu, Yaoyu
Kong, Xiangtao
Wei, Yuying
Meng, Dan
Zhang, Zhenxing
coredata.dc:description: As a global mental disorder, depression is associated with oxidative stress in the brain. Cysteine, a reductive biothiols, regulates the oxidative situation in many biological events including the stress that occurs in the tissues. Exploring the pathology and physiology of depression is still a challenge and always in an urgent need. Thus, developing a new method that could track Cys level without the interferes from other competing substances is of great importance. Herein, we developed a fluorescence probe that could selectively sensing Cys over other biothiols. Besides, we have demonstrated its desirable performance in cellular applications and mouse brain. This work provides a new method for Cys imaging and understanding pathogenesis of depression. We hope the work described here could be used as a potential chemical approach for the diagnosis of Cys associated diseases in clinical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Probe
Fluorescence
Cysteine
Imaging
Depression

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Spectral properties and selectivity of probe MCM

We also would like to thank the researchers from Peking Union Medical College for their assists and facilities. The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: General selectivity comparison of two probes. (a). Sensing behavior of probe MCM (5 μM). (b). Sensing behavior of probe PCM (5 μM). (c). Fluorescence intensity comparison. All measurements were performed in PBS buffer containing 0.5 % DMSO.
Sensing mechanism. (A). Proposed reaction with bio-thiols for probe MCM. (B). DFT calculation when sensing Cys. (C). Mass spectra of two different adduct products.

Spectral properties of probe MCM. (a). Fluorescence intensity response of MCM (10 μM) towards sensing Cys, GSH and Hcy (100 μM) at various pH values ranging from 3.0 to 10.0 (in PBS buffer containing 0.5 % DMSO). (b). Concentration-response of MCM (5.0 μM) at 494 nm with an increasing concentration of Cys in present. (c). Fluorescence intensity of the MCM (5 μM) at the emission wavelength upon the addition of increasing Cys concentrations (1–10 equiv.). Detect limit is also provided. (d). General selectivity of probe MCM towards biothiols and several common available amino acid & cations. (e). Interfere study of sensing property. (f). Time dependent study of probe MCM towards sensing Cys, GSH and Hcy.

Fluorescence imaging. (a1–a3) Images of HEK293 cells were first incubated with Cys (100 μM) 30 min and then treated with MCM (10 μM) for 20 min (b1-b3). Cells were treated with NEM (5 mM) for 10 min followed by treatment of probe MCM (10 μM) for 20 min (c1–c3). NEM treated cells were incubated with Cys (100 μM) 30 min followed by probe MCM (10 μM) for 20 min (d). Quantitative comparison of fluorescence intensity between three groups. Scale bar = 20 µm; Excitation wavelength: 405 nm; Green channel: 490–520 nm.

Fluorescence imaging of stress-induced depression mouse model. (A). Fluorescence imaging of mouse brain slice (100 µm) from three groups including normal mice as control, stress-induced 15 days and 30 days. First row: Images from blue channel (420–450 nm); Second row: Images from green channel (490–520 nm). (B). Quantitative comparison of fluorescence intensity from green channel between three groups. (***P < 0.001 compared to the control. Scale bar=50 µm).

Cysteine regulation and probe sensing mechanism. (A). Cys antioxidant mechanism. GSH and GSSG are cleaved by γ-glutamyl transpeptidase (GGT) and dipeptidases (DPs) to form Cys and Cys2. Cys2 is reduced by thioredoxin reductase 1 (Txnrd1) to Cys which protects cells from oxidization by eliminating ROS. Intracellular GSH is produced from its precursor amino acids which is catalyzed by glutamate–cysteine ligase (GCL) and GSH synthetase (GS). (B). Sensing mechanism of fluorescence probe.

Synthetic route to probe MCM/PCM. (i). ethyl nitroacetate, n-butanol, reflux 24 h, piperidine (cat.), (ii). SnCl2, 37 % HCl, 6 h room temperature. (iii). maleic anhydride, acetic anhydride, CHCl3, reflux 4 h.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence probe for selectively monitoring biothiols within cells and mouse depression diagnosis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a global mental disorder, depression is associated with oxidative stress in the brain. Cysteine, a reductive biothiols, regulates the oxidative situation in many biological events including the stress that occurs in the tissues. Exploring the pathology and physiology of depression is still a challenge and always in an urgent need. Thus, developing a new method that could track Cys level without the interferes from other competing substances is of great importance. Herein, we developed a fluorescence probe that could selectively sensing Cys over other biothiols. Besides, we have demonstrated its desirable performance in cellular applications and mouse brain. This work provides a new method for Cys imaging and understanding pathogenesis of depression. We hope the work described here could be used as a potential chemical approach for the diagnosis of Cys associated diseases in clinical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the global mental disorders, depression [1] exhibit several symptoms such as depressed mode, irritability, anhedonia and abnormalities in learning. Up to now, the pathology and physiology of depression is still unclear. Recent studies [2–4] suggested that depression could be caused by the over oxidative stress formed in the brain. Current diagnosis of depression [5] is mainly based on symptomatic criteria set forth which lacks the accuracy and timeliness. Thus, there is an urgent need in the development of novel method for the diagnosis of depression.

Cysteine (Cys), an essential amino acid, plays a key role ( Scheme 1A) in numerous physiological and pathological process [6] in biological systems. As one of the three most well-known sulfhydryl amino acid including glutathione (GSH) and homocysteine (Hcy), Cys is the production of cystine reduction and methionine demethylation. Despite its biological importance, the concentration of Cys has been used as a pivotal biomarker to diagnosis diseases in clinical applications. Today, numerous researches [7–10] have demonstrated that the lack of cysteine is associated with lethargy, edema, liver damage, weakness, slow growth and other health problems. In intracellular reduction process, cysteine acts as an important unit to protect cells from several oxidative related metabolisms [11,12]. Nevertheless, the variation of cysteine in the brains of depression mice model is still seldom studied due to the lack of efficient quantitative tools.

Fluorescence probe [13–16] has been widely applied in monitoring bioactive molecules in vitro and in vivo due to its non-invasive and high sensitivity. Numerous fluorescence probes have been constructed based on mechanisms [17–27] such as Michael addition, native chemical ligation, SNAr reaction, cyclization reaction, etc. In clinic, many diseases related physiological and pathological processes have been explored by this efficient method. To date, numerous outstanding probes [28–45] have been developed to track and monitor the molecular events, especially the biothiols [46,47]. However, one of the drawbacks to detect Cys was the interferes from other biothiols. Besides, signal to noise (S/N) ratio also affects the accuracy of the detection. Among all the current methods, Carcia and co-author reported a method based on HPLC to measure the level of cysteine which highly depends on a complicated instrument setup[48]. A silver nanocluster-based probe was reported for the detection of Cys was difficult to penetrate cells[49].

Recently, several intelligent probes [50–52] based on a CS strong electron-withdrawing structure were reported by Tang’s group. To meet the demand of detecting Cys in mice brain, we designed a fluorescent probe MCM that could selectively detect Cys with high precision. Coumarin was chosen as a fluorophore due to its excellent optical properties, good bio-compatible, ability to cross blood-brain barrier (BBB), low toxicity and convenient in synthesis. Maleimide group was chosen as acceptor due to its strong electron withdraw effect and fast response with thiol through Michael addition. In the absent of Cys, the probe MCM is non-fluorescent because of the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) from the fluorophore to the receptor. After Cys is added, the thioether formed by the Michael addition weaken the PET process and recover the fluorescent property of the probe (Scheme 1B). Our data demonstrated that this probe has the suitable selectivity, sensitivity, S/N ratio and bio-compatible for detection of Cys in the brain of stress-induced depression model mice.

All experiments were performed with commercially available reagents or solvents and used without further purification, unless otherwise noted. The probe MCM and PCM was prepared via the method (Scheme 1). The instruments and setup details utilized in this work were listed in the Supporting Information.

Compound 3 (70 mg, 0.256 mmol) and maleic anhydride (32 mg, 0.33 mmol) were dissolved in 20 mL of CHCl3. The mixture was heated to reflux for 3 h under nitrogen. After a bright orange precipitate formed, the solvent was removed under reduced pressure. Then, acetic anhydride (4 mL) and sodium acetate (150 mg) were added. The mixture was heated at 90 °C for another 1 h and then cooled to the room temperature. 200 mL of saturated NaHCO3 solution was poured into the mixture to neutralize reaction solution and the resulting solution was extracted with DCM. The organic phase was dried with anhydrous Na2SO4. The solvent was removed under reduced pressure and purified by column chromatography to give a pale-yellow solid (67 mg, 74.2 %). 1H NMR (400 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ 7.64 (s, 1H), 7.37 (d, J = 8.8 Hz, 1H), 6.89 (s, 2H), 6.84 (dd, J = 8.8, 2.4 Hz, 1H), 6.74 (d, J = 2.4 Hz, 1H), 3.88 (t, J = 4.9 Hz, 4H), 3.35 (t, J = 5.0 Hz, 4H). 13C NMR (101 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ 168.81, 158.13, 155.80, 154.38, 142.95, 134.80, 129.32, 113.42, 111.55, 109.90, 100.47, 66.38, 47.36. HRMS (ESI): calcd for C17H14N2O5 + [M+H]+: 326.0903; found: 326.0902. ( Scheme 2).

DFT calculation were performed by the Gaussian 09 program. All the geometrical structures were computed at B3LYP level in water by a polarizable model. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) were stimulated to propose the sensing mechanism of probe.

For all the spectral measurement, bio-compatible phosphate buffered saline (25 mM PBS) was used. Probes were first dissolved in DMSO for a stock solution (2.0 mM). The biothiols (GSH, Hcy, Cys, His, Glu, Phe, Arg, Val, Tyr, Ser, Gly, and Leu), cationic (K+, Mg2+, Ca2+), were all prepared using double distilled water at 20 mM for absorption and fluorescence spectrum measurements. The testing solution was shaken well and recorded after 10 min at room temperature, unless otherwise noted. Measurements of UV–vis absorption spectra (Quawell Q5000) were performed from wavelength 300–700 nm; measurements of fluorescence spectra (HITACHI F4600) were performed at excitation wavelength 378 nm and emission wavelength 490 nm at scan speed 800 nm/min with slit 5.0/5.0 nm. Each experiment was repeated three times. Quantum yield value was calculated by the quantitative comparison using quinine sulfate (Φ = 0.55 in 0.05 M H2SO4 aqueous solution) as a standard reference.

The vitro cytotoxicity of MCM to HEK293 cell lines were determined by a standard thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. Briefly, cells were seeded in 96-well culture plates at 3000 cells density per well. Then, cells were treated with fresh prepared MCM probe solution (1.0, 5.0 and 10.0 μM) and incubated for 24 h. After removal of the MTT solution, 150 μL DMSO was added to dissolve the purple formazan crystals. Absorbance at 570 nm was measured in a 96-well multi well-plate reader (Thermo-scientific). Each experiment was performed three times for accuracy. The cytotoxicity of the probe was determined by the ratio of the absorbance of the probe treated cells versus the control cells.
Cell viability= Asample / Acontrol *100 %
Where Asample is the absorption value of the probe treated group, Acontrol is the absorption value of control group.

For the confocal fluorescence imaging, living HEK293 cells were first cultured for 24 h in 6-well plates with glass bottom. Before taking imaging, cells were incubated with 100 μM Cys for 30 min separated into three groups. For group one, probe MCM (10.0 μM) was added and incubated for another 20 min before taking confocal images. For group two, cells were first treated with 5.0 mM NEM for 10 min and then probe MCM for another 20 min. For the last group, 50 μM Cys was added into one well from group two. Fluorescence was performed at 405 excitation wavelength and emission range were set at 450–550 nm. For data analysis, the average fluorescence intensity was obtained from region of interest (ROI) by program ImageJ. Experiment was repeated at least three times for the overall analysis. Data are presented as mean ± S.D.

Stress-induced depression model mice (∼10 weeks) were obtained and prepared by forced swimming test (FST) from Peking Union Medical College (n = 10). The normal Kunming mice were used as control group (n = 10). To image exogenous level of Cys, mice were first anesthetized by chloral hydrate, then 0.3 mg/kg Cys was injected in the abdominal area of each mouse. After 15 min, 0.2 mg/kg probe MCM was loaded by intraperitoneal injection. The mouse brain tissue was then treated with embedding agent and sliced by slicer (Leica CM1950) at 100 µm thickness before fluorescence imaging. All experimental procedures and protocol described in the study were approved and performed in accordance with the institutional animal guidelines of Peking Union Medical College.

First, we investigated the absorption of two probes, which centered at ∼368 nm with almost no fluorescence. When biothiols (100 μM) were added to probe MCM, obvious enhancement in fluorescence was observed ( Fig. 1a), especially for Cys (∼ 38-fold). However, there is no obvious change in the fluorescence for probe PCM when adding biothiols (Fig. 1b). To investigate the sensing mechanism, we proposed a pathway for the reaction involved. When Cys is added to the probe MCM, Michael addition reaction first occurred and formed a single bond between S and C atom which was confirmed by a peak at m/z: 448.1169 in HRMS. The same situation happened when GSH is added. For both situation, PET was restricted. However, in the case of Cys in present, -NH2 could further reacted with CO and formed NC bond which has weaker electron withdrawing effect which exists in a small portion (∼30 %) which was confirmed by a peak at 430.1049 in HRMS ( Fig. 2C). When only GSH in present, there will be no NC bond formed due to the steric hindrance for this situation. In other words, the final product of Cys involved has more restricted PET structure than that of GSH involved.

To demonstrate our hypothesis, we performed DFT calculation to investigate the photophysical properties of the MCM and its adduct product. As shown in Fig. 2B, the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) were displayed for probe MCM. The π electrons distributed on the maleimide region on the LUMO, while those on the HOMO were mainly remaining at the coumarin and morpholine region. Thus, strong PET process occurs from coumarin to maleimide region causing non-fluorescence of probe MCM. However, the π electrons of HOMO and LOMO are both restricted on the coumarin core that restricts PET process and recovered probe’s fluorescent performance. Next, we further tested the photo properties of probe MCM as well as its biological properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral properties and selectivity of probe MCM
ce:section.ce:para: All the photophysical properties probe MCM were measured under physiological conditions (PBS buffer containing 0.5 % DMSO) at room temperature, unless otherwise indicated. Fluorescence intensity of MCM maintained at a relatively high level between pH 6.0 and 8.0 ( Fig. 3a), which is much desirable for biological application. Besides, the interference of other complexes in the biological system can be avoided because of the good selectivity towards biothiols (Fig. 3d&e). The lower fluorescence signal in low pH buffers was due to the protonation of the aromatic amine, which affected the push–pull electronic system of the coumarin moiety. In the case of high pH buffers, high levels of hydroxyl in buffer may react with donor region and restricted the electron-pushing effect.

Encouraged by the above results, we decided to investigate more properties regarding the probe MCM. First, the response of the fluorescence intensity to various concentrations of Cys was tested, which exhibit a gradually increasing emission intensity (Fig. 3c) and a linear relationship was observed at the early stage. To test the specificity of the probe for Cys, we selected several common competing species (200 μM) such as amino acid and cations. As shown in Fig. 3d&e, the other biological species did not induce any significant increase in the fluorescence intensity. The time needed to achieve the maximum intensity is within 10 min (Fig. 3f), which is also desirable for biological detection. All the above data indicated that this probe is quite promising for further investigation into biological applications.

We next performed cell viability tests to determine if the probe MCM is a suitable fluorophore for biological system detection. A normal cell line (HEK293) was utilized in our study to conduct the MTT assay analysis. Cells were treated with different concentrations of the probe (from 1.0 to 10.0 μM) for 24 h before the addition of thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT). As shown in Figure. S1 (ESI†), the cell viability remained relatively high level, even at 10 μM. This has indicated probe MCM has low toxicity for cells and has potential application for detection in vitro and in vivo.

To verify the excellent response of MCM to Cys in living cells, HEK293 cell line was used in the confocal imaging. For the group a, cells were first incubated with Cys, then treated with probe MCM. We could observe a significant enhancement in the fluorescence intensity in the green channel ( Fig. 4a). On the contrary when cells were incubated with NEM (a thiol scavenger), the fluorescence intensity in group b decreased significantly (Fig. 4b), which indicated that fluorescence signal was blocked. To demonstrate the increased intracellular fluorescence was caused by Cys, same concentration of Cys was again added to the NEM treated group. As shown in Fig. 4c, fluorescence signal was recovered. As the probe itself has almost no fluorescence, reacting with biothiols (especially Cys) will undergo Michael addition and lead to a high fluorescence. The level of fluorescence in the group a was around 5-fold that of the NEM treated group. All the data indicated that MCM could be a promising candidate for monitoring Cys level in biological system.

Owing to its desirable performance in monitoring Cys within cellular applications, MCM could be applied to image biological events in vivo. We tested its capability to track the Cys level within the brains of normal mice and depression mice. For all the groups, mice were treated with 0.3 mg/kg Cys by intraperitoneal injection for 20 min followed by 0.2 mg/kg probe MCM injection. For the group that went through a 30-day stress, we observed an obvious decrease (∼1.8 fold) compared with that of the control group. However, the fluorescence level remains almost same in 15-day stress induced group and the control group. Above data indicate that the Cys level in long time stress mice is significantly reduced which may be due to the imbalance of the redox situation that occurred within the depression mouse brains. Overall, this probe could traverse the BBB and has potential usage in monitoring Cys in mouse brain which may have many clinical applications ( Fig. 5).

To conclude, we have prepared a novel fluorescence probe to track Cys in vitro and in vivo. The unique recognition site guarantees its good selectivity towards Cys over other biothiols (GSH and Hcy). Besides, we have demonstrated its perfect performance in monitoring Cys level in living cells as well as depression mouse brain. A negative correlation was observed between Cys levels and the degree of depression in mouse. We hope the design concept presented in this work could be further applied to create other fluorescent probes to distinguish biothiols as well as being used as a potential chemical tool for diagnosis of Cys associated diseases in clinical applications.
J.M., Y.X., X.K., D.M. and Y.W. Performed the experiments. J.M. and Z.Z. Conceived and supervised the project. J.M. and Z.Z. Wrote the manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by This work was financially supported by Henan Scientific Research Fund for Returned Scholars; National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 21572161); 510 Talent Programs (Jiangsu, China). We also would like to thank the researchers from Peking Union Medical College for their assists and facilities.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.biopha.2022.113647.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.J. Nestler, M. Barrot, R.J. DiLeone, A.J. Eisch, S.J. Gold, L.M. Monteggia, Neurobiology of depression, Neuron 34(1) (2002) 13–25.
V. Krishnan, E.J. Nestler, The molecular neurobiology of depression, Nature 455(7215) (2008) 894–902.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrogen sulfide protects neurons from oxidative stress

M.E. Anderson, A. Meister, Intracellular delivery of cysteine, Methods in enzymology 143 (1987) 313–325.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: New strategies for fluorescent probe design in medical diagnostic imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Kobayashi, M. Ogawa, R. Alford, P.L. Choyke, Y. Urano, New strategies for fluorescent probe design in medical diagnostic imaging, Chemical reviews 110(5) (2010) 2620–2640.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent probes for sensing and imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Ueno, T. Nagano, Fluorescent probes for sensing and imaging, Nature methods 8(8) (2011) 642–645.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in luminescent and colorimetric chemosensors for detection of thiols

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.S. Jung, X. Chen, J.S. Kim, J. Yoon, Recent progress in luminescent and colorimetric chemosensors for detection of thiols, Chemical Society Reviews 42(14) (2013) 6019–6031.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Discussions of fluorescence in selenium chemistry: recently reported probes, particles, and a clearer biological knowledge

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Abdillah, P.M. Sonawane, D. Kim, D. Mametov, S. Shimodaira, Y. Park, D.G. Churchill, Discussions of Fluorescence in Selenium Chemistry: Recently Reported Probes, Particles, and a Clearer Biological Knowledge, Molecules 26(3) (2021).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A coumarin-based biosensor for human hepatocellular carcinoma diagnosis with enhanced brightness and water solubility

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Ma, Y. Xu, X. Kong, Y. Wei, D. Meng, Z. Zhang, A coumarin-based biosensor for human hepatocellular carcinoma diagnosis with enhanced brightness and water solubility, Materials Chemistry Frontiers 5(20) (2021) 7548–7557.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A coumarin-based fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of thiophenols and its application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Li, C.-F. Zhang, S.-H. Yang, W.-C. Yang, G.-F. Yang, A coumarin-based fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of thiophenols and its application, Analytical chemistry 86(6) (2014) 3037–3042.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced doubly activated dual emission fluorescent probes for selective imaging of glutathione or cysteine in living systems

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.V. Mulay, Y. Kim, M. Choi, D.Y. Lee, J. Choi, Y. Lee, S. Jon, D.G. Churchill, Enhanced Doubly Activated Dual Emission Fluorescent Probes for Selective Imaging of Glutathione or Cysteine in Living Systems, Analytical Chemistry 90(4) (2018) 2648–2654.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared fluorescent probe for detection of thiophenols in water samples and living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Yu, F. Huang, S. Ding, G. Feng, Near-infrared fluorescent probe for detection of thiophenols in water samples and living cells, Analytical chemistry 86(17) (2014) 8835–8841.
